feedback.text: I am using this feedback collection session to bring up to important points: - the Stop Killing games initiative and what it entails. I'm using this opportunity to raise awareness for two issues: One, the Stop Killing Games Initiative and what it entails. Second, the recent attack of payment providers on freedom of expression on store platforms like Steam and itch.io. The Stop Killing Games initiative is focused on providing customers with the very basic and simple right of having a product that actually works, even when a publisher decides it is not worth their time anymore. This does not mean that online games have to be run indefinitely but instead giving players the option to run those games themselves, maybe on private servers; 15-20 years ago, this was a common practice for many people of my age. Video games are a form of digital art and thus, to put matters in the direction of the Digital Fairness Act, as a customer and EU citizen, I expect my (our) EU government to fight for digital products that keep working and are my very own property because I paid for them, just like I do for any other good. Secondly, VISA and Mastercard enforced video game storefronts to remove certain video games from their shelves with numbers up in the 1000s because they'd otherwise removed themselves as payment providers from said sites. This can't be lawfully and as a European customer and citizen, I expect the EU government to step up to these big payment companies. Some video games might not be to your taste and liking, which is to be expected. I personally didn't play or enjoyed 99,9% of these games either and yet this still is regulation of freedom of speech. Art doesn't have to be pretty or good for everyone; it doesn't have to cater for the tastes of a small minority of people who cannot understand that expression might look different for everyone. Even games that were pornographic should not be banned by the rules of VISA and Mastercard because it starts with those games, then do come politically involved games until we are all devoid of anything that has some level of personal touch or involvement. If anything, parents and these storefronts should be better informed (and inform others, like their kids etc.) about the games kids should and should not play. Anything else, may it be related to sexuality, religion and whatnot, is not up for somebody else to decide. So, please, consider protecting the freedom of expression when it comes to digital goods in this case video games as well. I'm glad that as a European I'm even able to give these remarks and write my feedback but I also hope it's not just a wall of text that stays unread. Lastly I would like to point out the anti consumer practices with live services games which encourage companies and developers to make gamble like games that crease short term profits and after the game has either failed or succeeded it gets closed and the company moves on on the next project. For me as a video game consumer its very bad since maybe my favorite live service game will bloat me with ads and payed advantages only to get a fully fledged product. For companies which have this business model they can anytime pull the plug on the game on the basis of not being profitable leaving people like me with a dead game and a lot of money spent on supporting it. All the best and thank you for your time and understanding
